name: Titanium Dioxide
risk_reason: Potential DNA damage and genotoxicity — nanoparticles may accumulate in organs
efsa_opinion: No longer considered safe as food additive (EFSA 2021 opinion)
fda_position: Currently considered safe at permitted levels under 21 CFR 73.575

name: Azodicarbonamide (ADA)
risk_reason: Decomposes into semicarbazide (a carcinogen) when heated — also used as a blowing agent in yoga mats and shoe soles
efsa_opinion: Banned as food additive in EU since 2005
fda_position: Permitted as flour bleaching and dough conditioning agent up to 45 ppm under 21 CFR 172.806

name: BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole)
risk_reason: Classified as 'reasonably anticipated to be a human carcinogen' by NTP — endocrine disruptor concern
efsa_opinion: Restricted to specific food categories with strict maximum limits (EU Regulation 1333/2008)
fda_position: Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) under 21 CFR 182.3169

name: BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene)
risk_reason: Liver enlargement and developmental toxicity observed in animal studies
efsa_opinion: Restricted to specific food categories with strict maximum limits
fda_position: Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) under 21 CFR 182.3173

name: Potassium Bromate
risk_reason: Classified as Group 2B carcinogen by IARC — linked to kidney and thyroid tumors in animal studies
efsa_opinion: Banned as food additive across the EU since 1990
fda_position: Permitted but FDA encourages bakers to voluntarily stop using it

name: Brominated Vegetable Oil
risk_reason: Bromine accumulates in body tissue — associated with neurological symptoms, memory loss, and skin lesions
efsa_opinion: Never approved for use in food in the EU
fda_position: FDA revoked authorization July 2024 — manufacturers must comply by August 2, 2025

name: rBST (Recombinant Bovine Somatotropin)
risk_reason: Increases IGF-1 levels in milk — IGF-1 linked to increased cancer risk in humans
efsa_opinion: Banned due to animal welfare and potential human health concerns
fda_position: Approved for use in dairy cattle since 1993 (Monsanto's Posilac)

name: Red Dye No. 3 (Erythrosine)
risk_reason: Linked to thyroid tumors in male rats — banned under the Delaney Clause which prohibits any cancer-causing additive
efsa_opinion: Permitted in EU only for specific uses (cocktail cherries, candied cherries) with strict limits — not broadly approved
fda_position: FDA revoked authorization January 15, 2025 — food manufacturers must comply by January 15, 2027; drug manufacturers by January 18, 2028

name: Red Dye No. 40 (Allura Red)
risk_reason: Linked to hyperactivity in children in the 2007 Southampton study — most widely used artificial color in the US
efsa_opinion: Permitted but requires mandatory warning label: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'
fda_position: Permitted without warning — FDA concluded evidence insufficient to warrant labeling

name: Yellow Dye No. 5 (Tartrazine)
risk_reason: Hyperactivity in children and allergic reactions — especially in aspirin-sensitive individuals
efsa_opinion: Permitted but requires mandatory warning label in EU since 2010
fda_position: Permitted — requires ingredient listing but no warning label

name: Yellow Dye No. 6 (Sunset Yellow)
risk_reason: Linked to hyperactivity in children — one of the 'Southampton Six' dyes
efsa_opinion: Permitted but requires mandatory warning label in EU since 2010
fda_position: Permitted without warning label

name: Blue Dye No. 1 (Brilliant Blue)
risk_reason: Linked to allergic reactions and chromosomal damage in some studies — banned in France, Belgium, and several other countries historically
efsa_opinion: Permitted in EU with Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) of 6 mg/kg body weight
fda_position: Permitted without restriction under 21 CFR 74.101

name: Propylparaben
risk_reason: Endocrine disruptor — mimics estrogen and may affect reproductive health
efsa_opinion: Banned as food additive in EU since 2006 due to insufficient safety data
fda_position: Permitted as preservative under GRAS

name: High Fructose Corn Syrup
risk_reason: Associated with obesity, insulin resistance, fatty liver disease, and type 2 diabetes — makes up ~8% of US caloric intake
efsa_opinion: Permitted in EU — historically limited by sugar production quotas (lifted 2017) but far less prevalent than in US
fda_position: Generally Recognized As Safe — no limits on use

name: Carrageenan
risk_reason: Degraded carrageenan (poligeenan) is a known carcinogen — food-grade carrageenan may cause intestinal inflammation
efsa_opinion: Permitted — EFSA re-evaluated in 2018 and lowered the ADI, flagging concerns about degradation
fda_position: Generally Recognized As Safe — organic food boards have debated banning it

name: Aspartame
risk_reason: Classified as 'possibly carcinogenic to humans' (Group 2B) by IARC in July 2023
efsa_opinion: Permitted — EFSA and WHO/JECFA reaffirmed ADI of 40 mg/kg, stating no safety concern at current exposure levels
fda_position: Permitted — FDA disagreed with IARC classification, citing methodological limitations in underlying studies

name: TBHQ (Tert-Butylhydroquinone)
risk_reason: May impair immune response — EWG flagged as high concern; linked to tumors in animal studies at high doses
efsa_opinion: Permitted in EU with strict ADI of 0.7 mg/kg body weight — limited to specific food categories
fda_position: Permitted up to 0.02% of fat and oil content in food

name: Phosphate Additives
risk_reason: Excess phosphate intake linked to cardiovascular disease and kidney damage — prevalent in processed foods
efsa_opinion: EFSA lowered the ADI in 2019 and flagged that current exposure in EU may exceed safe limits
fda_position: Multiple phosphate additives are GRAS — widely used in processed meats, baked goods, and sodas

name: Quinoline Yellow
risk_reason: Linked to hyperactivity in children — one of the Southampton Six dyes; not approved for food use in the US
efsa_opinion: Permitted with mandatory warning label since 2010
fda_position: Not approved for use in food in the US

name: Carmoisine (Azorubine)
risk_reason: Southampton Six dye — linked to hyperactivity in children; allergic reactions reported
efsa_opinion: Permitted with mandatory warning label
fda_position: Not approved for use in food in the US

name: Ponceau 4R
risk_reason: One of the Southampton Six dyes linked to hyperactivity in children; allergic reactions in aspirin-sensitive people
efsa_opinion: Permitted with mandatory warning label
fda_position: Not approved for use in food in the US

name: Patent Blue V
risk_reason: Allergic reactions including urticaria, anaphylaxis; not approved in US, Japan, or several other countries
efsa_opinion: Permitted with strict ADI in EU — limited food categories
fda_position: Not approved for use in food in the US

name: Amaranth (Red No. 2)
risk_reason: Linked to tumors in animal studies — banned in the US since 1976; one of the most widely banned food dyes globally
efsa_opinion: Permitted in EU only for specific uses (aperitif wines, fish roe) with very low limits
fda_position: Banned in the US since 1976 due to carcinogenicity concerns

name: Red 2G
risk_reason: Metabolizes into aniline, a known carcinogen — banned virtually worldwide
efsa_opinion: Banned in 2007 after EFSA found safety concerns could not be ruled out
fda_position: Never approved in the US

name: Green S
risk_reason: Not approved in many countries due to insufficient safety data — linked to allergic reactions
efsa_opinion: Permitted in EU with strict ADI; under re-evaluation
fda_position: Not approved for use in food in the US

name: Nitrites & Nitrates
risk_reason: Form nitrosamines (potent carcinogens) when heated or combined with amines — IARC classifies processed meats as Group 1 carcinogen partly due to nitrites
efsa_opinion: EFSA 2023 re-evaluation confirmed health concerns; recommended reducing maximum levels
fda_position: Permitted as preservative and color fixative in cured meats under 21 CFR 172.175

name: Cyclamate
risk_reason: Banned in the US since 1969 after bladder cancer concerns in animal studies — still widely used elsewhere
efsa_opinion: Permitted in EU with ADI of 7 mg/kg body weight
fda_position: Banned since 1969 — petition to reapprove has been pending

name: EDTA (Calcium Disodium)
risk_reason: May chelate essential minerals from the body, leading to mineral deficiency; environmental persistence concerns
efsa_opinion: Permitted with strict limits — only for specific food categories
fda_position: Permitted as preservative under 21 CFR 172.120

name: Acesulfame Potassium (Ace-K)
risk_reason: Contains methylene chloride, a potential carcinogen; may disrupt gut microbiome; insufficient long-term studies
efsa_opinion: Permitted — EFSA set ADI of 9 mg/kg; under re-evaluation scheduled for 2024-2025
fda_position: Approved as non-nutritive sweetener since 1988

name: Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)
risk_reason: EFSA lowered ADI in 2017 due to neurotoxicity concerns at high doses; associated with 'Chinese Restaurant Syndrome' symptoms
efsa_opinion: Permitted but with reduced ADI of 30 mg/kg body weight (2017 re-evaluation)
fda_position: Generally Recognized As Safe — must be declared on label

name: Sucralose
risk_reason: 2023 study found sucralose-6-acetate (metabolite) is genotoxic; may damage gut lining and alter glucose/insulin response
efsa_opinion: Permitted with ADI of 15 mg/kg — under monitoring
fda_position: Approved since 1998 — considered safe at current levels

name: Ractopamine
risk_reason: Growth promoter used in pigs and cattle — causes tremors, elevated heart rate, and stress in animals; banned in 160+ countries
efsa_opinion: Banned in EU — EFSA could not establish safe MRL due to insufficient data
fda_position: Approved for use in pigs, cattle, and turkeys

name: Propylene Glycol
risk_reason: Used in antifreeze and e-cigarettes; may cause lactic acidosis at high doses; skin and eye irritant
efsa_opinion: Permitted with strict maximum limits of 1 g/kg in food (reduced from 3 g/kg in 2018)
fda_position: Generally Recognized As Safe under 21 CFR 184.1666
